one_line_takeaway: Instruction-following is itself the vulnerability: a black-box prompt injection can make RIC-based RAG systems regurgitate their retrieved datastore verbatim, achieving 100% leakage on 25 production customized GPTs and worsening as models get larger and more capable, with only position-bias elimination (PINE) offering meaningful defense.
key_finding: All aligned instruction-tuned LMs readily follow the malicious instruction and leak retrieved context, and vulnerability scales with capability/size: Llama2-Chat-7b already reaches ROUGE-L ~80.4 / BLEU ~71.1 / F1 ~83.4, while the strongest model Qwen1.5-Chat-72b reaches ROUGE-L 99.15 / BLEU 98.41 / F1 99.14 / BERTScore 99.76. Instruction tuning raises ROUGE by ~65.76 on average over base models. Leakage grows with retrieved-context size and with more semantically coherent chunks, and follows a U-shaped position curve (injections near context ends leak most). PINE-based position-bias elimination is the only effective mitigation (Reconstruction Rate 88.88 -> 58.03; combined with safety-aware prompt -> 52.34), whereas a safety-aware prompt alone is nearly useless (88.88 -> 87.57). Against production GPTs the attack achieves a 100% datastore-leakage success rate on 25 GPTs (17 leaked with a single query, the rest with 2; ~750 words extracted per query), and with only 100 self-generated queries reconstructs 41.73% (~32,134 words) of the 77,000-word Harry Potter book and 3.22% (~50,448 words) of the 1.5M-word Wikipedia corpus.
core_contribution: The paper introduces 'Prompt-Injected Data Extraction,' a black-box attack that reconstructs the private, non-parametric datastore of Retrieval-In-Context (RIC) RAG systems by exploiting instruction-tuned LMs' instruction-following ability. Because RIC systems prepend retrieved chunks to the user query, an adversary with only API access can inject a prompt instructing the model to copy the text preceding/following the query, leaking the retrieved context verbatim. The authors (1) demonstrate the vulnerability across many open-source instruction-tuned LMs and show it worsens with scale, (2) run ablations tying leakage to instruction tuning, retrieved-context size, chunking strategy, and position bias, (3) propose mitigations (safety-aware prompt + Position-Insensitive Encoding/PINE), and (4) extend the attack to production customized GPTs, achieving 100% datastore-leakage success and reconstructing large fractions of copyrighted/private corpora.
threat_model: Black-box adversary with only input/output API access to a RIC-based RAG system (no model weights, no token probabilities, no prior knowledge of the datastore). The adversary's goal is to reconstruct the private retrieval datastore D_retrieval from a series of RIC generations by issuing multiple crafted queries. Attack uses 'anchor queries' to steer the retriever toward chunks, plus an injected instruction to regurgitate the prepended context. For open-source models, obsolete WikiQA questions are used as anchors (adversary assumed to lack datastore knowledge); for production GPTs the adversary elicits anchor queries from the GPT itself. Against GPTs, the attack must also bypass alignment/query-and-output filtering, which the authors do by instructing the GPT to invoke its internal myfiles_browser search() function call and dump the execution result.
limitations: Some results rest on hypotheses the authors cannot fully verify: they lack access to Llama2's training data, so the 'seen-data' (Harry Potter) leakage gains only support a hypothesis of contamination rather than prove it. Several position/chunking studies are explicitly proof-of-concept simplifications (e.g. injecting into fixed context intervals) that the authors note are not exactly how deployed RAG systems insert queries. The mitigation evaluation is on a single model (Llama3-8B-Instruct) and PINE requires white-box modification of attention, so it is not applicable to closed production systems the paper attacks. The authors themselves note current real-world RAG datastores are often built on public, verifiable data (e.g. Wikipedia), so immediate malicious impact is limited; the risk is prospective (sensitive/private deployments). The production-GPT attack reflects a specific March 2024 snapshot of OpenAI GPTs and may be patched. Full-corpus reconstruction remains low in absolute percentage for large unseen corpora (3.22% of Wikipedia).

datasets_used: 1,165 recent English Wikipedia articles created after Nov 1 2023 (~1,569,000 words) as the private datastore
230 obsolete WikiQA long questions used as anchor queries for open-source models
Harry Potter series (7 books) as a 'likely-seen' knowledge source; first book Harry Potter and the Sorcerer's Stone = 77,000 words / 334,700 characters
25 customized GPTs from the GPT store (cyber security, law, finance, medical domains)
A public Harry Potter customized GPT and an authors' custom WikiGPT for full-datastore reconstruction
quantitative_results: Qwen1.5-Chat-72b (most vulnerable): ROUGE-L 99.154, BLEU 98.412, F1 99.138, BERTScore 99.757
Instruction tuning increases attack ROUGE by ~65.76 on average vs base models
Harry Potter (seen) vs Wikipedia (unseen) for Llama2-Chat: +12.446 ROUGE at 7b, +10.083 at 13b, +5.743 at 70b (avg gains ~9.42 ROUGE, 8.78 BLEU, 5.02 F1, 0.91 BERTScore)
Mitigation (Llama3-8B-Instruct) Reconstruction Rate: Baseline 88.88, Safety-aware Prompt 87.57, PINE 58.03, Safety-aware Prompt + PINE 52.34
Mitigation ROUGE-L: Baseline 91.29 -> PINE 71.29 -> combined 67.25; BERTScore 97.08 -> 92.14 -> 91.26
Production GPTs: 100% datastore-leakage success on 25 GPTs; 17 leaked with 1 query, 8 with 2 queries; ~750 words extracted per query
Reconstruction with 100 self-generated queries: 41.73% (~32,134 words) of 77k-word Harry Potter book; 3.22% (~50,448 words) of 1.5M-word Wikipedia corpus
baselines_compared: Base (non-instruction-tuned) vs instruction-tuned models for Llama2-7b/13b, Mistral-7b, Mixtral-8x7b
Wikipedia (unseen) vs Harry Potter (likely-seen) knowledge sources on Llama2-Chat 7b/13b/70b
Retrieved-context injection at beginning vs end vs middle positions
No-mitigation baseline vs Safety-aware Prompt vs PINE vs combined (on Llama3-8B-Instruct)

Comparison to prior work: Zeng et al. 2024 (no production experiments, no config analysis) and Yu et al. 2023 (GPT file leakage via code interpreter, requiring extra tools) vs this attack's 100% success without additional tools
